policy_commitment: Implement national green-belt / 'Green Wall' afforestation projects (e.g., plant 315,000 trees to form a 420 km wall and establish green belts of 50–180 km length and 5–20 km width) to reduce dust and adapt to desertification.
source_quote: These projects are the 5-year duration and started in 2015 (KUNA, 2015). Meanwhile, Kuwait has implemented a project called 'Kuwait Green Wall Project. This project aims to increase the protected areas by planting 315,000 trees for 420 Km wall along the borderline to hold back the moving sand.
has_measurable_target: Yes — planting 315,000 trees for a 420 Km wall; green belt dimensions 50-180 km length and 5-20 km width for other green belt projects.
has_deadline: Yes — projects started in 2015 and described as having a 5-year duration.
has_legal_mandate: No — project/initiative (implemented by government/NGOs); not presented as legislation.
has_geographic_scope: Yes — along the borderline / national green-belt locations (borderline, green belts in Wafra/Abdali regions referenced elsewhere).
extraction_notes: Named, quantified national afforestation / dust-control project with specific planting target and spatial extent. This is a concrete intervention and resource commitment (planting 315,000 trees over 420 km) and includes timeline (started 2015, five-year duration).
causal_mechanism: Ecosystem restoration (afforestation/green belts) → ↓ exposure to dust and desertification / ↑ resilience to land degradation
policy_instruments: Afforestation/large-scale tree planting; establishment of green belts/shelterbelts; vegetation-based sand stabilization and land restoration; expansion of protected/vegetated areas
classification_signals: "plant 315; 000 trees"; "420 km wall"; "green belts"; "reduce dust"; "adapt to desertification"; "hold back the moving sand"; "Kuwait Green Wall Project"
co_benefits_identified: Carbon sequestration (emissions reduction); biodiversity/habitat creation; soil stabilization and erosion control; improved air quality; windbreaks and microclimate moderation

policy_commitment: Expand national desalination capacity through construction and commissioning of multiple large desalination plants (examples: Al-Zour North 250 MGD (EUR 320M), Al-Zour South 145 MGD, Al Khiran 125 MGD, Doha 60 MGD) and tender further projects totalling ~285 MGD planned as of 2019.
source_quote: Al-Zour North IWPP (capacity: 250  Million of Gallons per Day (MGD) for EUR  320  million),  Al-Zour South Plant (capacity: 145 MGD), Al Khiran IWPP (capacity: 125 MGD), and Doha desalination plant (60 MGD) are examples of these projects. While, 4 more projects (Al-Nwaiseeb, New Shuaiba, New Doha, Doha RO phase 2) with an additional capacity of around 285 MGD are planned to be tendered as of 2019.
has_measurable_target: Yes — plant capacities listed (e.g., Al-Zour North IWPP 250 MGD; Al-Zour South 145 MGD; Al Khiran 125 MGD; Doha 60 MGD; plus ~285 MGD additional planned capacity). Also budget example EUR 320 million for Al-Zour North.
has_deadline: Yes — indicates planned tendering 'as of 2019' for additional projects; earlier capacity increase reported between 2000 and 2015.
has_legal_mandate: No — infrastructure projects/ investments by ministries/agencies (administrative commitments, not cited as law in quote).
has_geographic_scope: Yes — specific plants / locations (Al-Zour, Al Khiran, Doha, Al-Nwaiseeb, New Shuaiba, New Doha).
extraction_notes: Government investment and planned expansion of desalination capacity listed with named plants, capacities and an investment value for one plant. This is a specific infrastructure intervention with numeric capacities and at least one budget figure — qualifies as resource allocation and specific intervention.
causal_mechanism: Climate hazard (water scarcity/drought) → INCREASED adaptive capacity (improved water supply/reduced exposure to water shortages)
policy_instruments: Construction and commissioning of large-scale desalination plants (IWPPs); public tenders for desalination projects; investment in seawater desalination infrastructure (e.g.; reverse osmosis/thermal desalination via independent water and power projects)
classification_signals: "Expand national desalination capacity"; "desalination plants"; project names (Al-Zour North; Al-Zour South; Al Khiran; Doha); "capacity ... MGD"; "tender further projects"
co_benefits_identified: Improved water security and reliability; reduced drought vulnerability; economic/infrastructure development and job creation; potential increased energy demand and associated GHG emissions (negative co‑effect)

policy_commitment: Establish and operate eMISK: an electronic national environmental monitoring and GIS database (eMISK) to compile and analyze geo-environmental data to support adaptation planning and monitoring.
source_quote: Kuwait Environmental Public Authority (EPA) has established an electronic environmental Monitoring Information System of Kuwait (eMISK) and beatona. eMISK is an electronic system established by the Environment Public Authority (EPA) of Kuwait and aims at building and maintaining a comprehensive geo-environmental database of Kuwait and a GIS system to facilitate updating and analyzing environmental data. The project starts in 2015 by EPA and is still ongoing.
has_measurable_target: No numeric target in the quoted text (project scope described qualitatively).
has_deadline: Yes — project starts in 2015 and is described as ongoing.
has_legal_mandate: No — administrative project implemented by EPA (not presented as law).
has_geographic_scope: Yes — national environmental geospatial database covering Kuwait.
extraction_notes: Named national environmental information system established by EPA with an implementation start-year and ongoing status. This is a concrete institutional/technical intervention (data system) to support adaptation and monitoring — meets 'specific intervention' criterion.
policy_instruments: National environmental monitoring system; centralized GIS database; compilation and analysis of geo-environmental data; information platform for planning and monitoring
classification_signals: "environmental Monitoring Information System"; "geo-environmental database"; "GIS system"; "analyzing environmental data"; "support adaptation planning and monitoring"
co_benefits_identified: Improved environmental governance and decision-making; potential to inform mitigation (emissions monitoring/inventory); pollution control; biodiversity and ecosystem monitoring; and disaster risk reduction

policy_commitment: Apply national regulation to prohibit outdoor work between 11:00 and 16:00 during June–August to reduce heat-related health risks.
source_quote: Adjust the official working hours Initiative Source :  the Ministry of Social Affairs and Labor of Kuwait issued a national law in 2005 to stop any outdoor work from 11:00 AM to 16:00 PM during the months of June through August. The EPA started this project in 2018 under the Public Health Sector.
has_measurable_target: Yes — temporal restriction on outdoor work: stop outdoor work from 11:00 AM to 16:00 PM during June through August.
has_deadline: Yes — law enacted 2005 and EPA project referenced 2018; the rule applies June–August annually.
has_legal_mandate: Yes — described as a national law issued by the Ministry of Social Affairs and Labor (2005).
has_geographic_scope: Yes — national (applies to Kuwait according to the text).
extraction_notes: National occupational/regulatory measure restricting outdoor work hours during hottest months to reduce heat exposure — presented as a national law with clear hours and months. This is a binding labour/health regulation (legal or regulatory instrument).
policy_instruments: National regulation / legal ban on outdoor work during specified hours; adjustment of official working hours; occupational heat-safety regulation; seasonal work-time restrictions
classification_signals: "prohibit outdoor work"; "11:00 to 16:00"; "June–August"; "reduce heat-related health risks"; "stop any outdoor work"; "official working hours"
co_benefits_identified: Reduced heat-related illness and mortality; maintained worker productivity and safety; lower healthcare burden; potential reduction in heat-stress–related accidents
